title: Wave Interference Laboratory
description: Simulate and visualise wave interference patterns for single-slit, double-slit, and diffraction grating experiments. Reproduce Young's famous double-slit result.
whyItMatters: Wave interference is the phenomenon that proved light is a wave, and later revealed the quantum nature of matter. Understanding interference patterns is essential for optics, quantum mechanics, and modern technology like holography and thin-film coatings.

steps.title: Single slit diffraction
steps.description: Simulate a single slit by summing contributions from many point sources across the slit width. Plot the intensity pattern on a distant screen.
steps.tips: The single-slit pattern has a central maximum twice as wide as the side maxima.

steps.title: Young's double slit
steps.description: Simulate two narrow slits and compute the interference pattern. Vary slit separation and wavelength to see how the fringe spacing changes.
steps.tips: Fringe spacing = lambda * L / d, where L is screen distance and d is slit separation.

steps.title: Diffraction grating
steps.description: Simulate N slits (N = 2, 5, 10, 50, 1000) and observe how the pattern sharpens as N increases — explaining why gratings produce sharp spectral lines.
steps.tips: With N slits, there are N-2 secondary minima between each principal maximum.

steps.title: 2D interference
steps.description: Extend to 2D: simulate circular wave interference from two point sources (like ripples in a pond). Create an animated visualisation.
steps.tips: Use imshow with a time-varying phase to create a mesmerising animation.

steps.title: White light through a prism
steps.description: Simulate a diffraction grating illuminated by white light (sum of all visible wavelengths). Show how the grating disperses white light into a rainbow spectrum.

deliverables: Single-slit, double-slit, and grating intensity plots
Analysis of how fringe spacing depends on wavelength and geometry
Comparison to Young's original experimental results

rubric.description: Superposition and phase differences correctly implemented
Patterns match theoretical predictions for all configurations
Quantitative comparison of fringe spacing to theory
